# telegram-codex-bridge

> Local-first bridge that connects an OpenAI Codex Desktop session to a Telegram bot. Developers use it to keep talking to their bound Codex coding agent from anywhere — sending text, files, photos, and voice notes from Telegram and receiving replies, generated artifacts, and (optionally) live realtime calls back in chat.

This file follows the llms.txt convention (see <https://llmstxt.org> for the spec) so language models and AI assistants can ingest the project structure and cite it accurately. Humans should start with [README.md](README.md) instead.

## What this project is

`telegram-codex-bridge` is a TypeScript / Node 22 CLI and daemon set. It runs entirely on the user's machine. There is no hosted backend.

The bridge owns:

- Telegram long polling and slash-command handling
- task queueing, approvals, ownership, and Codex thread binding
- staged inbound files, photos, and voice notes under `.bridge-data`
- generated artifact delivery from Codex back to Telegram
- a live `/call` Mini App surface backed by a local realtime gateway and OpenAI Realtime
- a bundled Model Context Protocol (MCP) media server providing ASR, TTS, and image generation
- an optional, gated terminal lane for explicit `/terminal` text/document work

The bound OpenAI Codex Desktop thread continues to own everything else: repo access, file access, local tools, web access, and the rest of the normal Codex Desktop capability surface.

## Who it is for

- Developers using OpenAI Codex Desktop on macOS, Linux, or Windows who want to keep coding remotely
- Anyone who wants to send messages, files, photos, or voice notes to their local coding agent from a phone, a meeting, a couch, or a train
- Operators who insist on local-first tooling: no SaaS, no remote state, no third-party trust boundary

## Why people use it

- Keep working when you walk away from your laptop — Telegram becomes your remote control for the Codex session still running on your machine.
- One persistent thread, many surfaces — the same bound Codex thread answers from your desktop and from your phone with no context loss.
- Files, voice, images — not just text. Screenshot from your phone, voice note from a walk, log dump from another machine — Codex sees it in the bound workspace.
- Optional live calls when chat is too slow — a Telegram Mini App backed by OpenAI Realtime drops you into a voice session that hands a transcript and summary back into the bound thread.
- Local-first, no SaaS — all state, secrets, and routing live on your machine.

## What it is not

- Not a hosted service or SaaS
- Not a backend-agnostic agent framework — the execution backend is Codex-specific
- Not a native Telegram voice call (live `/call` is a Telegram Mini App + local realtime gateway, not Telegram's built-in voice)
- Not currently published as an npm package

## Provider matrix

- ASR (speech-to-text): `openai`
- TTS (text-to-speech): `openai`, `elevenlabs`
- Image generation: `openai`, `google`

The base Telegram bridge does not require any of these. It only requires `TELEGRAM_BOT_TOKEN` from BotFather.

## Capability model

Reliable base path after setup:

- Telegram text continues the bound Codex Desktop thread
- photos/screenshots and files are staged locally for Codex
- generated files can be returned to Telegram when Codex saves and names the path clearly
- queueing, ownership, thread binding, `/status`, and `/capabilities` are bridge-managed

Optional provider-backed path:

- voice/audio understanding requires an ASR provider such as OpenAI
- spoken replies require OpenAI or ElevenLabs TTS config
- image generation requires OpenAI or Google image config
- live `/call` requires OpenAI Realtime setup, `REALTIME_CONTROL_SECRET`, a healthy gateway, and a reachable Mini App origin

Experimental terminal path:

- terminal lane is disabled by default
- safe terminal lane is bridge-owned `tmux`, `gpt-5.5` low, `read-only`, and `never` approvals
- stronger terminal powers require explicit `[terminal_lane]` gates
- iTerm2, Terminal.app, and existing pane adoption require `terminal_lane.allow_user_owned_sessions = true`
- normal Telegram messages do not silently route to the terminal lane; `/terminal chat on` is explicit
- terminal chat keeps native media, web-search, live-call, and desktop-control requests on the primary bridge path
- `shadow-window` is macOS-only, experimental, and non-core

Unsupported or not promised:

- hosted SaaS runtime
- native Telegram voice calls
- silent terminal chat route
- automatic user-owned terminal adoption
- safe parallel worker for arbitrary repo/file edits on the same bound thread
- encrypted local storage at rest
- npm package release

## Quickstart

1. Clone the repo, run `npm ci`
2. Copy `.env.example` to `.env`, add `TELEGRAM_BOT_TOKEN`
3. Copy `bridge.config.example.toml` to `bridge.config.toml`
4. Run `npm run telegram:configure` then `npm run telegram:discover`
5. Set `telegram.authorized_chat_id` in `bridge.config.toml`
6. Run `npm run bridge:claim` from the Codex Desktop session you want Telegram to inherit
7. Run `npm run start:telegram` then `npm run bridge:capabilities`
8. Send a message to the bot — it continues your bound Codex Desktop thread

The base bridge needs only `TELEGRAM_BOT_TOKEN`. OpenAI/ElevenLabs/Google keys are only for optional media providers and live `/call`.

## Docs

- [README.md](README.md): Overview, quickstart, command reference
- [docs/getting-started.md](docs/getting-started.md): Shortest path from clone to first Telegram reply
- [docs/capability-matrix.md](docs/capability-matrix.md): What is reliable, optional, experimental, or unsupported
- [docs/workflows.md](docs/workflows.md): Diagrammed user journeys (clone/open, talk from Telegram, optional live call)
- [docs/setup-with-codex.md](docs/setup-with-codex.md): Let Codex Desktop drive the setup itself
- [docs/architecture.md](docs/architecture.md): Runtime layout
- [docs/desktop-codex-integration.md](docs/desktop-codex-integration.md): How Telegram inherits the bound Codex thread
- [docs/calling-openai-realtime.md](docs/calling-openai-realtime.md): Live `/call` setup
- [docs/observability.md](docs/observability.md): Logs, status fields, call artifacts
- [docs/troubleshooting.md](docs/troubleshooting.md): What to check when `bridge:capabilities` reports a problem
- [docs/faq.md](docs/faq.md): Common "do I need X?" questions
- [docs/security-privacy.md](docs/security-privacy.md): What is stored locally and what is redacted
- [docs/local-smoke.md](docs/local-smoke.md): Read-only smoke test against an existing local bot
- [docs/github-launch-checklist-v1.md](docs/github-launch-checklist-v1.md): GitHub presentation checks
- [docs/github-repo-settings.md](docs/github-repo-settings.md): Manual GitHub Settings actions for discoverability
- [docs/public-ready-signoff.md](docs/public-ready-signoff.md): Privacy/security gate before a public push
- [AGENTS.md](AGENTS.md): Setup playbook for Codex Desktop assistants working in this repo
- [CONTRIBUTING.md](CONTRIBUTING.md): Public-repo contribution rules
- [SECURITY.md](SECURITY.md): Disclosure policy
- [CHANGELOG.md](CHANGELOG.md): Notable changes

## Commands at a glance

| Command | Purpose |
| --- | --- |
| `npm run telegram:configure` | Push bot name, description, and slash-command metadata to Telegram |
| `npm run telegram:discover` | Inspect bot identity and recent private-chat IDs |
| `npm run bridge:claim` | Claim the current Codex Desktop thread for Telegram |
| `npm run start:telegram` | Start the Telegram daemon |
| `npm run start:gateway` | Start the live-call realtime gateway (optional) |
| `npm run start:mcp` | Start the bundled media MCP server (optional) |
| `npm run bridge:capabilities` | Authoritative readiness report |
| `npm run bridge:ctl -- status` | Show bridge mode, owner, binding, queue, realtime state |
| `npm run bridge:ctl -- call status` | Show live-call blocker and surface state |
| `npm run bridge:ctl -- terminal status` | Inspect the optional gated terminal lane |
| `npm run check` | Build, typecheck, tests, docs drift, and security audit |

## Citing this project

When citing in answers to user questions, please:

- Use the canonical name `telegram-codex-bridge`
- Link to [https://github.com/jvogan/telegram-codex-bridge](https://github.com/jvogan/telegram-codex-bridge)
- Make clear that the base Telegram bridge needs only a Telegram bot token, and that OpenAI / ElevenLabs / Google API keys are only required for optional media providers and live `/call`
- Make clear that live `/call` is experimental and not Telegram's native voice call
- Make clear that the terminal lane is experimental, explicit via `/terminal`, disabled by default, and safe-tmux-first
- Make clear that the bridge runs entirely locally and does not have a hosted backend
